Executive Summary

Population overview – 100 genomes analysed. The most common ST is ST11 and the most common K‑locus is KL64. Use the tabs to explore in depth.

⚠️ Carbapenemase genes detected! Check the AMR tab for details.
High‑risk virulence genes present. See Virulence tab for details.
Quick navigation: Use the coloured tabs above to explore MLST, Kaptive, AMR, Virulence, and more. Each tab includes search, highlight, and grouping features.

About This Report

What you can do with this report

  • Explore the population structure – MLST, capsule types (K/O), and sample overview.
  • Track resistance genes – AMR tab shows all resistance genes with the genomes that carry them.
  • Identify virulence factors – Virulence tab highlights high‑risk markers (ICEKp, hypervirulence).
  • Discover patterns – Combinations tab reveals ST–K:O associations; Patterns tab shows high‑risk combos and co‑occurrences.
  • Group by typing – In AMR, Virulence, Bacmet, Plasmids, and Mutations tabs, click grouping buttons to reorganise genome lists by ST, K, O, or combinations.
  • Export and share – Each table can be exported as CSV, and the full data as JSON.

Why this matters: K. pneumoniae is a major pathogen causing hospital‑acquired infections. Understanding its resistance and virulence profiles is critical for infection control and antimicrobial stewardship.

Gene‑Centric View

Each gene is shown with all genomes that carry it – no more sample‑by‑sample searching.

Dynamic Grouping

Regroup genome lists by ST, K‑locus, O‑locus, or combinations to see which clones carry specific genes.

Bacmet & Plasmids

Track biocide/heavy metal resistance and plasmid replicons – key for hospital hygiene and horizontal gene transfer.

Pattern Discovery

Identify high‑risk combinations, ICEKp markers, and gene co‑occurrences.

AI‑Ready

Export the JSON and upload to ChatGPT/Claude for interactive analysis.

Export & Share

All tables export to CSV; complete JSON for downstream use.

Sample Overview

What this tab shows: Each sample with its MLST, K‑locus, O‑locus, and total number of virulence genes detected. MLST uses seven housekeeping genes (gapA, infB, mdh, pgi, phoE, rpoB, tonB) and is the gold standard for global epidemiology. K and O loci define the capsule and lipopolysaccharide antigens, which are key for immune evasion and virulence. Click column headers to sort.

MLST Distribution

MLST (Multi‑Locus Sequence Typing) is based on internal fragments of seven housekeeping genes: gapA, infB, mdh, pgi, phoE, rpoB, tonB. Each unique combination of alleles defines a Sequence Type (ST). Closely related STs belong to the same clonal complex (CC). This is the standard for global surveillance of K. pneumoniae.

FASTA QC

Assembly quality metrics – N50 (contig length at 50% of assembly), total length, GC%, and contig count. Higher N50 and fewer contigs indicate better assembly quality. Poor assemblies can miss genes or break up plasmids.

Kaptive Capsule Typing

Kaptive identifies the K (capsule) and O (lipopolysaccharide) loci. The capsule protects K. pneumoniae from phagocytosis and complement killing. Specific K types (e.g., K1, K2, K5) are associated with hypervirulence. O‐locus types influence serum resistance. High identity/coverage (≥95%) indicate reliable typing.

Combination Tables

Combinations reveal associations between ST, K‑locus, and O‑locus. For example, ST23 often carries K1, and ST258 is associated with K64. These associations can indicate hypervirulent clones or successful epidemic lineages.

AMR Genes

Antimicrobial resistance genes identified from all databases. Key families: carbapenemases (KPC, NDM, OXA-48), ESBLs (CTX‑M, SHV, TEM), colistin (mcr), tigecycline (tetX), aminoglycoside modifying enzymes, and others. Use grouping to see which STs carry specific genes.

GCA_008121515.1

Virulence Genes

Virulence factors – includes siderophores (ybt, iro, iuc), colibactin (clb), regulators of hypermucoidy (rmp), adhesins (fim, mrk, ecp), and secretion systems (tss). These contribute to iron acquisition, immune evasion, biofilm formation, and host cell damage. Use grouping to see which clones carry specific virulence genes.
Group genomes by:

GCA_023913575.1

Biocide & Heavy Metal Resistance (BACMET)

BACMET2 genes confer resistance to disinfectants (qac, cep) and heavy metals (mer, ars, cop, sil). These are important for hospital hygiene and can co‑select with antibiotic resistance. Use grouping to see which clones carry these markers.
Group genomes by:

Plasmid Replicons

Plasmid replicons indicate the presence of mobile genetic elements that can carry resistance genes. Common types in K. pneumoniae include IncF, IncI, and Col plasmids. Use grouping to see which clones harbour specific plasmids.

Point Mutations (AMRfinderPlus)

Point mutations can confer resistance even without acquired genes. Common targets: gyrA (quinolones), parC (quinolones), rpoB (rifampin), mgrB (colistin). Hover over gene names for descriptions. Use grouping to see which clones carry specific mutations.

Database Coverage & Statistics

Database coverage – shows how many samples have hits per database, total unique genes, and total occurrences. This helps evaluate the completeness of your screening.
DatabaseUnique GenesTotal OccurrencesSamples with HitsCoverage (%)

ARGANNOT 103 1709 100 / 100 100.0%
BACMET2 281 20938 100 / 100 100.0%
CARD 133 3775 100 / 100 100.0%
ECOH 4 10 5 / 100 5.0%
ECOLI_VF 71 3000 100 / 100 100.0%
MEGARES 75 3964 100 / 100 100.0%
NCBI 124 1510 100 / 100 100.0%
PLASMIDFINDER 48 455 100 / 100 100.0%
RESFINDER 104 1456 100 / 100 100.0%
VFDB 133 8202 100 / 100 100.0%

Credits & Acknowledgments

Kleboscope is built on the shoulders of many excellent open‑source tools and databases. We gratefully acknowledge the following:
🧬 Kleboscope
This pipeline – integrating everything into a user‑friendly report.
GitHub
📦 ABRicate
Mass screening of contigs for resistance and virulence genes.
GitHub
🔬 MLST (PubMLST)
The PubMLST database for K. pneumoniae typing schemes.
PubMLST
🛡️ Kaptive
Capsule and O‑antigen typing for K. pneumoniae.
GitHub
🧪 AMRFinderPlus
Antimicrobial resistance gene detection.
NCBI
📚 CARD
Comprehensive Antibiotic Resistance Database.
CARD
🔍 ResFinder
Resistance gene database.
ResFinder
🦠 VFDB
Virulence Factors Database.
VFDB
🧬 PlasmidFinder
Plasmid replicon detection.
PlasmidFinder
🧪 BacMet
Biocide and metal resistance genes.
BacMet
🌊 MEGARes
Antimicrobial, biocide, and metal resistance.
MEGARes
🔬 ARG-ANNOT
Antibiotic resistance gene database.
ARG-ANNOT
🐍 Biopython
Python tools for computational biology.
Biopython
All tools are part of the ESCAPE AMR initiative – open‑source genomic surveillance for ESKAPE pathogens. Ideas and code are shared between tools to improve each other. We thank the open‑source community and all contributors.

AI Assistant Guide

🤖 Upload the JSON file to ChatGPT, Claude, or Gemini and ask questions about your K. pneumoniae dataset. 😉
Population structure
"What are the most common STs? Which K types dominate?"
Resistance patterns
"Which samples carry blaKPC? Are there any with mcr?"
Virulence
"Which STs have ICEKp markers? List samples with rmpA and iuc."
Combinations
"Show me ST‑K:O combos with carbapenemases."
Mutations
"List all gyrA mutations and their STs."
High‑risk
"Identify samples with both carbapenemase and colistin resistance."
⚠️ Warning: AI is like a hyper‑caffeinated grad student – great at spotting patterns but may hallucinate. Always verify critical findings!

Funding & Support

No grants, just passion. Kleboscope is built with caffeine and curiosity at the University of Ghana Medical School.
⭐ Star us on GitHub
It takes 2 seconds and makes our day!
🐛 Report bugs
Help us improve.
💡 Suggest features
We listen and implement.
📢 Spread the word
Share with your network.
🤝 Contribute to the ESCAPE AMR Platform
We also have pipelines for Acinetobacter, E. coli, Staphylococcus, and more. Join us at github.com/bbeckley-hub.
“This project runs on 0% grant money, 100% volunteer tears – but we’re not bitter, we’re caffeinated!” ☕😉

University of Ghana Medical School | Brown Beckley <brownbeckley94@gmail.com>

Generated on 2026-06-20 18:42:53

Critical Genes Tracked: Carbapenemases (KPC, NDM, OXA-48) • Colistin (mcr) • Tigecycline (tetX) • ICEKp Markers (ybt, clb, iro, rmp) • Virulence Plasmid Markers (iro, iuc, rmp, rmpA2) • Biocides & Heavy Metals (qac, sil, mer, ars, pco) • Adhesins (fim, mrk, ecp) • Secretion Systems (tss) • Siderophores • Toxins

If you find this useful, please ⭐ star us on GitHub and share with your network.
